<!-- Generated by the Analysis-tab state-of-the-art review workflow (run wf_7a526974-700), 2026-06-08. Review-only; no application code. -->

# review:engineering

## Strengths to keep

- **Clean service boundary & purity.** `runAnalysisQuery` is the single entry point; `aggregate`/`scopeResolver`/`monitoring` are pure and clock-free, and `factFetch` is the only Supabase-touching module. This is exactly the invariant-#2/#6 architecture and makes the in-memory→SQL swap (below) feasible without touching the UI. (`src/lib/analysis/runAnalysisQuery.ts`, `aggregate.ts`, `factFetch.ts`)
- **Planned/performed never conflated.** Separate `planned`/`performed` fact subsets per bucket, delta/adherence derived by comparison in `computeMeasureValues`. (`aggregate.ts:217-256`)
- **Unit-safe tonnage.** `loadIsKg`/`loadIsPct` flags + `unresolvedPctFacts` accounting prevent kg/% mixing (T-02). (`factFetch.ts:162-202`, `metricRegistry.ts:99`)
- **Graceful query repair.** `validateAnalysisQuery` drops unknown metrics/axes and repairs `viz.yAxis` so a stale saved view degrades instead of throwing. (`validate.ts`)
- **viz-only changes correctly avoid refetch.** `useRunQuery`'s `key` deliberately omits `viz`, so table↔chart switches are instant and don't re-hit Supabase. Confirmed correct. (`useRunQuery.ts:21-28`)

## Gaps vs state-of-the-art

### 1. No table virtualization; chart guard doesn't protect the table
- **Why it matters:** `PivotTable` renders every `rowKeys × (colKeys × measures)` cell to the DOM. A `date × exercise` pivot over a macro for a group is easily 200 dates × 20 exercises × 4 facets = tens of thousands of `<td>` nodes. The `MAX_SERIES`/`tooManyBars` guard in `ResultChart` protects charts but **not** the table — the table is the fallback the Overload message tells coaches to use, yet it's the one with no ceiling. (`PivotTable.tsx:113-145`, `ResultChart.tsx:53-64`)
- **Concrete change:** Add row virtualization to `PivotTable` (the codebase already does smooth large-list work in the planner; reuse that pattern rather than adding a dep if possible, else `@tanstack/react-virtual`). At minimum, add a cheap guard: if `rowKeys.length * leaves.length > N` render a "narrow scope / X rows" banner with a "render anyway" affordance, mirroring `Overload`. Heatmap (`HeatmapGrid`) has the same unbounded-grid exposure.
- **Files:** `PivotTable.tsx`, `HeatmapGrid.tsx`, `ResultChart.tsx`. **Impact:** H. **Effort:** M.

### 2. `toChartModel` / lookup maps / `resolveMeasures` are recomputed every render
- **Why it matters:** `ResultChart` calls `toChartModel(result)` (and `toChartModel(compare)` + `mergeCompare`) on **every render**, not just when `result` changes — each call rebuilds a `Map` over all records and a full `data[]`. `PivotTable`, `HeatmapGrid`, and `resultToCsv` each rebuild their own `lookup` Map over `result.records` on every render too. `aggregate` also calls `resolveMeasures(query, registry)` fresh each run (cheap, but it's duplicated logic with `computeMeasureValues`' key derivation). (`ResultChart.tsx:42-45`, `PivotTable.tsx:52-53`, `HeatmapGrid.tsx:19-20`)
- **Concrete change:** Wrap `toChartModel`/`mergeCompare` in `useMemo([result, compare, type])` inside `ResultChart`; memoize the `lookup` Map in `PivotTable`/`HeatmapGrid` with `useMemo([result])`. Better: build the `lookup` once in the adapter layer (or attach an index to `AnalysisResult`) so table/chart/heatmap/CSV share one map instead of four. Consider `React.memo` on `PivotTable`/`ResultChart` since `AnalysisModule` re-renders on every rail keystroke (`set(...)`), and these children currently re-run their full build each time even when `result` is referentially identical.
- **Files:** `ResultChart.tsx`, `PivotTable.tsx`, `HeatmapGrid.tsx`, optionally `vizAdapter.ts`. **Impact:** M-H. **Effort:** S.

### 3. `availableValues` distinct-scan is O(facts × dims) and runs on every aggregate
- **Why it matters:** After bucketing, `aggregate` re-scans the **entire unfiltered** fact set once per filterable dimension (`rows ∪ cols ∪ {exercise,category,movement,weekType}`), calling `dimValues` (which allocates arrays) per fact per dim. With a wide pivot that's several extra full passes over facts on every query run — and it runs even when the rail's filter UI for those dims isn't open. (`aggregate.ts:424-434`)
- **Concrete change:** Compute distinct values in the **same single pass** that builds buckets (accumulate into per-dim `Set`s while iterating `filtered`/`facts` once), or only compute it for dimensions the builder actually exposes as filters. The per-dim 500-cap `break` is also order-dependent (it can drop legitimate values for a high-cardinality dim before reaching others) — switch to a per-dim cap that doesn't abort the whole scan. **Note:** it scans `facts` (unfiltered) which is intentional, but a `date` row dim will blow the cap with calendar dates that aren't even offered as filters.
- **Files:** `aggregate.ts`. **Impact:** M. **Effort:** S-M.

### 4. Dual-axis unit assignment silently mis-assigns when >2 distinct units
- **Why it matters:** `axisIdFor` maps `unit === units[0] → left`, everything else → `right`. With 3+ units (e.g. tonnage kg + reps + %1RM + adherence %) all non-first units collapse onto the single right axis and flatten against each other — the exact failure the comment says it's preventing. There's no guard or note. (`ResultChart.tsx:77-80, 134-135, 155-156`)
- **Concrete change:** If `units.length > 2`, surface a note ("3 unit types on 2 axes — values may not be comparable") or restrict the right axis to the second unit only and warn for the rest. Long-term, group series by unit and only render the dominant two, or offer per-series axis assignment.
- **Files:** `ResultChart.tsx`. **Impact:** M. **Effort:** S.

### 5. `mergeCompare` positional alignment breaks when periods have different row counts
- **Why it matters:** `mergeCompare` aligns ghost series by **index** (`compare.data[i]`). If the current period has 8 weeks but the prior period (different scope, possibly with empty-bucket pruning in `aggregate`) yields 7 rows, every ghost point is shifted by one — "week 1 vs prior week 1" silently becomes "week 1 vs prior week 2". Empty-bucket pruning (`aggregate.ts:402`) makes mismatched lengths likely, not rare. (`vizAdapter.ts:98-114`)
- **Concrete change:** Either (a) align by relative position within a *dense* period axis (pad both models to the full week/date count before merging), or (b) document the constraint and skip the overlay with a note when `base.data.length !== compare.data.length`. Given the snap-to-Monday + pruning interactions, (a) is the correct fix.
- **Files:** `vizAdapter.ts`, possibly `aggregate.ts` (option to not prune for chart). **Impact:** M. **Effort:** M.

### 6. Snap-to-Monday window filter can drop weeks that would snap into range
- **Why it matters:** `factFetch` filters `week_plans` with `.gte('week_start', isoMonday(window.from))` but buckets by `snapToMonday(wp.week_start)`. `isoMonday` always goes to the *start* of the ISO week; `snapToMonday` goes to the *nearest* Monday (Thu–Sun snap forward). A `week_start` stored as a corrupt Thu/Fri just below `isoMonday(window.from)` would be excluded at the DB but would have snapped to a Monday inside the window. The performed stream filters on `date` while planned filters on `week_start`, a second asymmetry. (`factFetch.ts:267, 416, 684-686`)
- **Concrete change:** Widen the planned DB filter by ~3 days (`isoAddDays(isoMonday(window.from), -3)`) to cover the snap radius, then rely on the in-memory snap+bucket to be authoritative; or store/query a snapped column. Add a fixture test with a Thursday/Sunday `week_start` near the boundary.
- **Files:** `factFetch.ts`, `engine.test.ts`. **Impact:** M (data-correctness, silent). **Effort:** S.

### 7. Normalization edge cases when athlete dim absent / filters empty
- **Why it matters:** `applyNormalization` correctly returns a note when `athlete` isn't a row/col. But `perAthleteMean` divides by an athlete's own mean computed *across the current result* — if a normalization is requested with a single result row per athlete, every value becomes exactly 100 (mathematically correct but useless), with no note. Separately, `perBodyweight` keys bodyweight by **display name** (`athleteBodyweight[a.name]`), so two athletes with the same name silently collide and same-named athletes can't be distinguished. (`aggregate.ts:322-344`, `factFetch.ts:574-578`)
- **Concrete change:** Note the degenerate single-point-per-athlete case. Key bodyweight by `athleteId` and have `applyNormalization` resolve via the id→name map rather than name (the engine groups by id everywhere else — the name keying here is an inconsistency).
- **Files:** `aggregate.ts`, `factFetch.ts`. **Impact:** M. **Effort:** S.

### 8. Empty/all-filters and `custom:` metric handling
- **Why it matters:** (a) An empty `in` filter is dropped in `buildQuery` (`builderState.ts:102`) — good — but `aggregate.matchesFilter` for a raw `in` with `values: []` would exclude everything; the engine relies on the builder to sanitize, so any other caller (presets, drill, saved views, tests) can footgun. (b) `custom:<id>` appears in the `Dimension` type and `dimValues`/`dimNumber` handle it, but there is **no `custom:` metric path** — the registry has no custom metric extractor, so a `custom:` *measure* silently resolves to `null`. The prompt explicitly flags `custom:` metrics; today only the *dimension* side works. (`aggregate.ts:110-125`, `types.ts:33,168`, `metricRegistry.ts`)
- **Concrete change:** Make `matchesFilter` treat empty-`in` as "no constraint" defensively in the engine. Decide and document whether `custom:<id>` measures are in scope; if so, add a base-metric factory that reads `row.custom[id]`; if not, have `validate` drop `custom:` measure ids with a warning so they don't silently vanish.
- **Files:** `aggregate.ts`, `validate.ts`, `metricRegistry.ts`. **Impact:** M. **Effort:** S-M.

### 9. Too-many-series guard vs Top-N auto-cap (UX)
- **Why it matters:** The current guard is binary: past 24 series (or 240 bars) it shows an Overload wall and renders nothing. For a coach this is a dead end — the most useful chart (top 8 exercises by volume) is one they can't get without manually adding filters. (`ResultChart.tsx:53-64, 176-188`)
- **Concrete change:** Replace/augment with a Top-N auto-cap: rank series by aggregate magnitude, keep top N (e.g. 12), fold the rest into "Other" (or just hide with a "+N more — add a filter to see all" caption). Keep the hard wall only as a last resort. This also answers the table question: the table should *not* use the series guard (it scales differently) — it needs the row-virtualization/row-count guard from finding #1 instead.
- **Files:** `ResultChart.tsx`, `vizAdapter.ts` (Top-N selection belongs in the adapter so legend/colors stay consistent). **Impact:** M-H (UX). **Effort:** M.

### 10. Test coverage is engine-only — filters, availableValues, chart adapter, monitoring-view untested
- **Why it matters:** `engine.test.ts` covers buildFacts/aggregate/scope/validate/normalization/monitoring math well, but there are **zero tests** for: `matchesFilter` (in/eq/between, empty-in, multi-valued group), `availableValues` (cap behavior, date explosion), `toChartModel`/`mergeCompare` (series keys, dual-unit, ghost positional alignment — finding #5), `resultToCsv`, and `combo expansion edge cases` beyond the one happy-path test. `useRunQuery`'s debounce/stale-guard has no test.
- **Concrete change:** Add adapter tests (`vizAdapter`), filter/availableValues tests in the engine suite, and a `mergeCompare` mismatched-length test that locks in the fix from #5. The pure layers make all of this trivially testable — the gap is just unwritten tests, not architecture.
- **Files:** new `__tests__/adapter.test.ts`, extend `engine.test.ts`. **Impact:** M. **Effort:** M.

### 11. In-memory aggregation ceiling — when SQL/RPC is needed
- **Why it matters:** `factFetch` pulls every set-line and every log-set into the client and `aggregate` builds a `Map` keyed by `JSON.stringify([rowCombo, colCombo])` per fact per combo (`aggregate.ts:381-391`) — `JSON.stringify` per bucket-write is the hot path. For one athlete over 8 weeks this is fine; for a group of 20 over a full macro (thousands of sessions × sets) this is hundreds of thousands of rows transferred + stringified client-side, plus the multiple full passes from finding #3. The architecture is correct for the swap, but there's no ceiling instrumentation. (`factFetch.ts` whole file, `aggregate.ts:369-392`)
- **Concrete change:** (a) Replace `JSON.stringify` bucket keys with a delimiter-joined string of the already-string axis values (combos are `string[]`), avoiding the serialize cost. (b) Add a `factCount` soft cap / telemetry note in `meta` so you know when you're approaching the ceiling. (c) Plan the SQL swap behind `runAnalysisQuery`: a Postgres RPC / materialized fact view becomes worthwhile once group-over-macro is a common query (rough trigger: >~50k facts or >2s aggregate). The `factFetch` per-stream fetch order is already a sane blueprint for that view.
- **Files:** `aggregate.ts`, `factFetch.ts`, `types.ts` (meta). **Impact:** H at scale, L today. **Effort:** S (key swap) / L (RPC).

### 12. Type-safety / debt
- **Why it matters:** Pervasive `as Array<{...}>` / `as RawX[]` casts on every Supabase `.select()` in `factFetch` (lines 527, 540, 571, 592, 602, 611, 618, 634, 648, 651, 687, 698, 711-712, 734, 741, 750) — these are unchecked and will silently produce `undefined` fields if a column is renamed. `unit: 'absolute_kg' as string | null` (`factFetch.ts:432`) is an odd cast. `cyclesByAthlete[athleteId]` uses `typeof cycles` as a type (`factFetch.ts:655`) which is brittle. The `stress` metric is a registered-but-always-null slot (`metricRegistry.ts:144-159`) — intentional, but it's selectable and always shows "—", which will read as a bug to coaches; consider hiding it from the measure picker until the model lands.
- **Concrete change:** Generate row types from Supabase (`mcp__supabase__generate_typescript_types`) and type the `.select()` results instead of casting; or define one `select`-shaped interface per table and assert once. Hide `stress` from `DEFAULT_VISIBLE`/picker or label it "(coming soon)".
- **Files:** `factFetch.ts`, `metricRegistry.ts`. **Impact:** L-M. **Effort:** M.

## Open questions

- Are `custom:<id>` athlete day-card values intended as **measures** (aggregatable, e.g. avg RPE/readiness) or only as **grouping dimensions**? Today only the dimension side is wired; the measure side silently yields null (finding #8).
- What's the realistic worst-case query size in production (largest group × longest macro)? That sets whether finding #11's SQL swap is near-term or theoretical.
- Should empty-bucket pruning (`aggregate.ts:402`) be disabled for chart/period-over-period rendering so the x-axis is dense and `mergeCompare` alignment is well-defined (finding #5)?
- Is `nl` (number of lifts = `isCompetitionLift || movement != null`) the agreed definition, given the registry comment flags a future dedicated "counts as NL" flag (T-10)? This is a domain sign-off, not an engineering call.
